title: The Disproportionate-Assets Case: A Continuing Story! (3)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the complete 2005–2011 adjournment chronology, exact hearing dates, quoted judicial rebukes, attributed allegations, genuine English passages, the Kodanadu precedent, and the serialized ending. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which the corrupted duplicate series marker and only clear OCR character, date, spacing, and scan-fragment errors are repaired. The canonical source remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: When the trial was transferred to Bengaluru, the case was already nearing completion. A judgment was expected within a few months. Yet, because of the extraordinary skill of the accused in obtaining adjournment after adjournment, it has been dragged out for ten years.
On 28 March 2005, the witness statements and case documents were translated into English and supplied to all the accused and to the Tamil Nadu Government.

Even when the disproportionate-assets trial against Jayalalithaa and three others began in Bengaluru after a delay of two years, Jayalalithaa did not appear before the Special Court. Her side sought one month's time. Special Judge Pachhapure refused and adjourned the case to 18 April 2005. On the eighteenth, neither Jayalalithaa nor Sasikala appeared. Jayalalithaa's lawyer too was absent. An assistant to senior counsel for the accused said that the senior advocate was unwell and in hospital.
“Let him remain absent. Where are the five accused? I shall deliver judgment based on the accused's failure to appear,” the judge warned sternly.

Nine years have passed since the judge said this on 18 April 2005, yet judgment has still not been delivered. That is the curious spectacle presented by our administration of justice. The judge finally adjourned the hearing to 9 May 2005 and ordered Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and all the others to appear without fail.

When the case came up on 9 May, Jayalalithaa's senior counsel K.T. Tulsi sought a lengthy adjournment. Judge Pachhapure became furious because Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and the other accused had not appeared. “I grant one final opportunity on the sixteenth. I will grant no more time,” he said, ordering all five to appear that day. When their lawyers asked for an adjournment, do you know what Judge Pachhapure said?

“A request for a long adjournment cannot be accepted. I intend to hear the case from day to day. If adjournments are sought so often, how can the case be tried quickly? You may have work in many courts, but can this case be postponed for the sake of one lawyer? That is unreasonable. The case has remained pending for many years. Repeated requests for time cannot be accepted. Is it right to drag the case out for two and a half years through adjournments and now seek another? The Supreme Court has directed me to conduct this case expeditiously. There has been no hearing for the past six months. I have sat alone in this courtroom for six months. I feel as though I have been placed in solitary confinement.”
That was how Judge Pachhapure expressed his anguish.

Yet no one appeared on the sixteenth either. Instead, the AIADMK lawyer demanded copies of the charge sheet filed in the London assets case and said the trial could proceed only afterwards. The judge replied: “It is clear that you are asking for the charge-sheet copies in the London assets case solely to drag out this case. Why did you not ask for them during all the earlier requests for adjournment? Asking now is unreasonable. Nevertheless, I shall supply the copies immediately. Will you be ready to argue tomorrow?”

When the copies were immediately supplied under the judge's order, Jayalalithaa's lawyers said they ran to 1,800 pages and sought a three-week adjournment. Refusing three weeks, the judge adjourned the hearing to 25 May 2005.

Meanwhile, the matter came before the Bengaluru Special Court on 27 June 2005. The Court accepted a petition the first accused, Jayalalithaa, had filed and left pending before the Chennai Special Court on 11 February 2002. It sought to combine the disproportionate-assets case with the London hotel-purchase case and try them together. The Bengaluru court ordered a joint trial. In July 2005, our General Secretary, the Professor, petitioned the Supreme Court against combining the two cases. The Supreme Court initially stayed the Special Court proceedings, but later ordered that the disproportionate-assets trial could continue.
It is necessary to remember how the accused continued to act without respecting legal procedure in order to prolong the trial indefinitely.

After more than four years of delay, the accused petitioned on 3 March 2010 to alter an order summoning forty-two prosecution witnesses. The trial court dismissed the petition on 5 March. In the same month, they petitioned the Karnataka High Court against the framing of charges by the Chennai Special Court on 5 June 1997. The Karnataka High Court dismissed that petition on 10 March 2010.

The accused then approached the Supreme Court on 19 March. Following the Supreme Court's guidance, the trial court revised the schedule and fixed 3 May 2010 for recommencement of the trial. On 18 April, the accused filed another petition claiming that the investigating officer had not been duly authorised, that the entire investigation was therefore contrary to law, and that the whole trial must be stopped. The trial court dismissed it on 27 April. The accused filed a Special Leave Petition in the Supreme Court. When the Supreme Court said they could approach the High Court under Section 482 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, the petition was withdrawn.

On 7 May 2010, the first accused, Jayalalithaa, requested that the trial court adjourn the case because a related matter was to be heard by the Supreme Court on 11 May. The case was adjourned. That May, she petitioned for three sets of copies of the documents found in the 70,000-page charge sheet. They were supplied as requested on 26 June 2010.

On 15 July, Jayalalithaa filed a petition saying she did not require the copies translated into English. The third accused, Sudhakaran, filed the same petition on 21 July. On 22 July, the request was rejected with certain directions. Jayalalithaa therefore appealed to the Karnataka High Court on 29 July.
Although witnesses were present in court, the accused sought adjournments. The court fixed 6 August, 9 August, 11 August and 13 August 2010 for examination of witnesses, but it did not take place.
Because Jayalalithaa's appeal concerning the translated copies was pending in the Karnataka High Court, the Special Court had to keep postponing the trial.

At the hearing on 19 October 2010, the accused again sought an adjournment. The trial court rejected the request and ordered witnesses to appear for re-examination on 16 November. On that day, the accused again asked for postponement. The court rejected the request and adjourned the hearing to 18 November.

On 18 November, the accused said they had filed an appeal in the Karnataka High Court and that the High Court had ordered the trial court proceedings postponed. The trial court therefore adjourned the case to 25 November.

On 23 November, the Karnataka High Court rejected the accused's request. Its order was sent to the trial court on 25 November, and the trial court adjourned its hearing to 30 November. On 30 November, the accused said they intended to appeal the Karnataka High Court's order to the Supreme Court. The case was adjourned to 15 December.

On 15 December 2010, two witnesses were re-examined and cross-examined. Three were re-examined and cross-examined on 16 December; four on 3 January 2011; and two on 4 January. The defence claimed there were errors in the translation of the testimony and sought time to prepare and submit a correct translation. The trial court adjourned the hearing to 18 January 2011.

On 18 January, the defence sought an adjournment because the father of one of its lawyers had died. The request was rejected and five witnesses were examined. The defence then sought postponement of cross-examination, which was also refused.

On 19 January, three witnesses were examined. The defence again asked to postpone cross-examination, and that request too was rejected. On the same day, the Special Public Prosecutor recorded that he had completed the prosecution's submissions. The defence sought time to read the evidence and was given one week.

As I have described, the accused kept securing adjournment after adjournment to delay the trial without limit. Finally, because of the Special Judge's firm action, there was no alternative and the Public Prosecutor began compiling and reading the witness testimony.

A Public Prosecutor must argue against the accused. When Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case was tried in Tamil Nadu under an AIADMK government, the Supreme Court itself recorded in its 2003 judgment how the Public Prosecutor acted in sympathy with the accused and transferred the case to Karnataka. Yet the DMK found that Bhavani Singh, appointed Public Prosecutor by the Karnataka Government on 2 February 2013, was arguing in support of the accused in the same fashion followed under the AIADMK administration in Tamil Nadu.

The DMK therefore demanded his removal. The Karnataka Government accepted the allegation and removed Bhavani Singh from office. But Jayalalithaa's side, the accused, went all the way to the Supreme Court arguing that Bhavani Singh, who was supposed to argue against them, must remain Public Prosecutor and that retiring Judge Balakrishna must continue to hear the case. Those who believed in legal principles were astonished and shocked by this strange spectacle. Retired Judge Balakrishna did not agree to continue, and Judge John Michael D'Cunha is now hearing the matter.
Jayalalithaa's side going to the Supreme Court to demand that Judge Balakrishna alone continue this case reminds me of another incident, which I have mentioned before.

On 4 April 2008, the Supreme Court delivered a judgment concerning the “Kodanadu Estate,” reported in *The Hindu*. The manager of the estate had erected a gate on a road long used by the public in the Nilgiris district and prevented anyone from passing. Members of the public petitioned the Revenue Divisional Officer, the Sub-Divisional Magistrate, asking that the obstruction be removed. He ordered that the gate be removed within twenty-four hours, failing which action would be taken under the Indian Penal Code.
The Kodanadu Estate manager challenged this order in the Madras High Court. The case was first assigned to one judge, who was unable to take it up, after which it was transferred to another.

Jayalalithaa and Sasikala's side objected to the transfer before the Chief Justice of the High Court. The Chief Justice ruled that assignment of judges fell within the Chief Justice's exclusive authority. Did they leave it there?

The Kodanadu Estate manager appealed to the Supreme Court. The matter came before the Chief Justice of India on 4 April 2008. The Chief Justice said the manager's request lacked merit and that such matters could not be entertained. When counsel claimed it was a bona fide petition, the Chief Justice said: “It is bereft of bona fides; we will not entertain such matters.”

Addressing counsel for the estate, the Chief Justice asked: “What do you want? You are Bench hunting. The Chief Justice of the High Court knows many things which you may not know. You have absolutely no right to say that your matter should be heard by a particular Judge.”
Some imagine that the people of Tamil Nadu will have forgotten these words, steeped in the essence of law and justice and worthy of being remembered forever.

In the Bengaluru disproportionate-assets case too, the demand that one particular judge must continue hearing the matter was rejected, and a new judge is conducting the trial. When Jayalalithaa's request that Judge Balakrishna alone continue the case failed in the Supreme Court, everyone believed that the law was performing its duty splendidly.
(To be continued)
With affection,
M.K.
